serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Seafood Masala, often served with rice or bread, is approximately 250 grams.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research - National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from 'Fish Curry' and 'Prawn Curry' entries in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods to represent a mixed seafood masala, considering the base gravy components of onion, tomato, and spices.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Indian Foods — analogous estimate from protein-rich Indian curries
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI estimated at 50 based on similar protein-rich, low-starch Indian curries (e.g., chicken curry without rice) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The primary carbohydrate contributors are onion, tomato, and spices; seafood itself contributes negligible glycemic load. GL per serving = (50 × 5.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 6.9.

hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be mindful of the sodium content (~350 mg per 100 g), which can accumulate significantly in a full serving; request reduced-salt preparations and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seafood Masala has a low estimated glycemic index (~50) and is high in protein (~12.5 g per 100 g), making it a suitable main-course option for blood glucose management when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (~2.5 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~60 mg per 100 g); individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean seafood preparations, limit added cooking oil, and monitor total saturated fat intake across the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood Masala is a flavorful Indian curry dish featuring a medley of braised prawn and fish cooked in a rich, aromatic gravy of onions, tomatoes, ginger, garlic, and a blend of traditional Indian herbs and spices such as cumin, coriander, turmeric, and chilli. The dish typically has a medium-thick consistency, offering a savory and mildly spicy taste profile with tender, succulent pieces of seafood that absorb the complex spice base. It is a popular main course in Indian restaurants across Singapore and South Asia, often served with steamed basmati rice, naan bread, or roti. Nutritionally, Seafood Masala is a good source of lean protein (~12.5 g per 100 g) from the prawn and fish, with moderate fat (~7 g per 100 g) and low carbohydrates (~5.5 g per 100 g) primarily from the vegetable base and spices. The dish has a low estimated glycemic index (~50), making it suitable for individuals managing blood sugar levels. It is generally gluten-free and dairy-free depending on the specific recipe, and is considered halal when prepared without alcohol or pork-derived ingredients. Sodium content (~350 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for those managing hypertension.
common_user_queries: Is seafood masala healthy for diabetes?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from similar Indian fish and prawn curry dishes documented in ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods. Glycemic index is an analogous estimate from protein-rich Indian curries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Multilingual names and ontology codes are well-researched with appropriate closest-parent designations.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for the analogous dishes. polyunsaturated_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. trans_fat_g is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source. All other reported nutrient values are derived from adapted ICMR-NIN data for Fish Curry and Prawn Curry entries. Water_g is an estimate based on typical curry composition. Halal status assumes preparation without alcohol or pork-derived ingredients as served at the listed restaurant.
